source.title: A seamless and iterative DNA assembly method named PS-Brick and its assisted metabolic engineering for threonine and 1-propanol production

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The paper addresses the need for a DNA assembly method that is iterative, seamless, and capable of assembling repetitive sequences, to facilitate DBTL cycles in metabolic engineering for threonine and 1-propanol production. Existing methods like BioBrick (Type IIP REs) leave scars and are not seamless, while Golden Gate (Type IIS REs) lacks reusability. PS-Brick combines Type IIP and IIS REs to achieve iterative, seamless, and repetitive sequence assembly.

section_gists.heading: A seamless and iterative DNA assembly method named PS-Brick and its assisted metabolic engineering for threonine and 1-propanol production
section_gists.gist: Title page with authors and affiliations.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: PS-Brick is a Type IIP and IIS RE-assisted method for iterative, seamless, and repetitive sequence assembly. One round takes hours, with high efficiency (10^4-10^5 CFU/μg DNA) and accuracy (~90%). Applied to threonine production via DBTL cycles, achieving 45.71 g/L threonine. Also used for CRISPR array construction and 1-propanol pathway assembly (1.35 g/L).
process_signals: Method development driven by need for iterative, scarless, and repetitive sequence assembly in metabolic engineering.
Application to threonine production involved stepwise DBTL cycles: feedback regulation release, bottleneck elimination, export intensification, catabolism inactivation.
Seamless cloning enabled codon saturation mutagenesis and bicistronic design.
Repetitive sequence cloning enabled tandem CRISPR sgRNA arrays.
Heterologous 1-propanol pathway assembled in one cycle.
methods: PS-Brick: combines Type IIP and IIS REs, uses PCR fragments and purified plasmids.
Applied to E. coli for threonine and 1-propanol production.

section_gists.heading: Background
section_gists.gist: Metabolic engineering requires iterative DBTL cycles, needing iterative DNA assembly methods. Existing RE-based methods: BioBrick (Type IIP) leaves scars, not seamless; Golden Gate (Type IIS) limited reusability. MoClo/Golden Braid enable iterative but complex. PS-Brick aims to combine strengths of both RE types for iterative, seamless, and repetitive assembly.
process_signals: Motivation: limitations of current methods—BioBrick scars (8 nt) hinder protein fusions; Golden Gate not reusable; MoClo/Golden Braid complex.
Gap: no method combines iterative, seamless, and repetitive assembly simply.
Hypothesis: using both Type IIP and IIS REs can achieve these properties.

section_gists.heading: Design and proof-of-concept of PS-Brick assembly scheme The design principles of PS-Brick
section_gists.gist: PS-Brick uses Type IIP (SphI) and Type IIS (BmrI for TA cloning, MlyI for blunt end ligation) REs. Vectors pOB and pOM created with entrance sites. PCR products digested with SphI, ligated to vector. Assembly efficiency 4-8×10^4 CFU/μg DNA, accuracy 88.2-95.1%. Seamless joints confirmed. Workflow completed in half a day. Comparison with traditional cloning showed similar efficiency. Blunt end ligation allows complete seamless assembly; TA cloning offers more RE options. Self-ligation of undigested vector reduced accuracy; dephosphorylation suggested.
process_signals: Design hypothesis: combine Type IIP and IIS REs to achieve iterative and seamless assembly.
Proof-of-concept: constructed vectors pOB (BmrI/SphI) and pOM (MlyI/SphI).
Tested cutting efficiency: BmrI and MlyI cut efficiently in 15 min, but uncut plasmid background required longer digestion (30 min) to reduce transformation.
SphI digestion time (15-180 min) did not affect assembly efficiency or accuracy significantly.
Accuracy ranged 88.2-95.1%; failures due to self-ligation of vector not fully digested by SphI.
Blunt end/MlyI-based PS-Brick is completely sequence-independent, preferred for universal application.
TA clone-based PS-Brick has more RE options (BmrI, BciVI, HphI).
Optimization needed: dephosphorylation to prevent self-ligation.
methods: PS-Brick assembly: vector pOB/pOM double-digested with SphI and BmrI/MlyI; PCR products digested with SphI; ligation.
TA cloning (BmrI) and blunt end ligation (MlyI) variants.
Transformation into E. coli DH5α.
Assembly efficiency: CFU/μg DNA.
Accuracy: sequencing of 20 clones.
metrics: Cutting time: 15 min sufficient for REs
Workflow time: half a day for one round
failures_or_limits: Uncut plasmid background reduced transformation efficiency; resolved by longer digestion (30 min).
Self-ligation of vector due to incomplete SphI digestion; suggested dephosphorylation.
Blunt end ligation expected less efficient but similar due to enzyme choice.

section_gists.heading: Optimization of the PS-Brick
section_gists.gist: Cutting efficiency of BmrI and MlyI tested: 15 min sufficient, but transformation test showed uncut plasmids reduced over time; 30 min used. SphI digestion time (15-180 min) gave similar assembly efficiency (~4-8×10^4 CFU/μg DNA) and accuracy (88.2-95.1%). Workflow time: half a day. Comparison with traditional SphI/XbaI cloning showed similar efficiency. Blunt end ligation (MlyI) allows complete seamless assembly; TA cloning (BmrI) has more RE options. Self-ligation of vector identified as cause of incorrect clones; dephosphorylation recommended.
process_signals: Optimization: tested cutting times for BmrI and MlyI; 15 min enough but 30 min used to reduce uncut plasmid.
SphI digestion time did not affect efficiency/accuracy.
Self-ligation issue: clones without insert had original vector sequence, indicating incomplete SphI digestion.
Solution: dephosphorylation to prevent self-ligation.
Blunt end/MlyI-based PS-Brick is completely seamless and sequence-independent.
TA clone-based PS-Brick offers more RE choices.
methods: Gel electrophoresis and transformation tests for cutting efficiency.
Assembly efficiency and accuracy measured as before.
Comparison with traditional cloning using SphI/XbaI.
metrics: Workflow: half a day per round
failures_or_limits: Uncut plasmid background: resolved by 30 min digestion.
Blunt end ligation efficiency similar to cohesive end due to enzyme choice.

section_gists.heading: DBTL cycles for pathway engineering for the production of threonine and its derivate 1-propanol through PS-Brick
section_gists.gist: PS-Brick applied to threonine overproduction via iterative DBTL cycles: release feedback regulation, intensify biosynthesis, eliminate rate-limiting steps, inactivate catabolism, modify transport, reinforce coenzyme regeneration. Four rounds of DBTL cycle performed stepwise through six cycles of PS-Brick reactions, gradually increasing threonine production.
process_signals: Application strategy: stepwise DBTL cycles using PS-Brick.
General metabolic engineering strategy for threonine outlined.
Four DBTL rounds performed, each resolving one aspect.
methods: PS-Brick used for sequential assembly of genetic modifications.
DBTL cycles: design, build, test, learn.

section_gists.heading: Site-directed mutagenesis for feedback-resistant ThrA
section_gists.gist: Seamless PS-Brick used for codon saturation mutagenesis of ThrA residue 433 to relieve feedback inhibition. 20 mutants generated in one cycle. ThrA-Gly433Phe gave 48-fold higher threonine than wild-type. Vector pOthr created with HindIII/MlyI entrance sites after removing internal MlyI sites. Seamless in-frame fusion enabled precise mutagenesis.
process_signals: First DBTL cycle: release feedback regulation.
Design: saturation mutagenesis at Gly433 of ThrA using seamless PS-Brick.
Vector preparation: removed four MlyI sites from pACYC184 to create pOthr.
20 PCR donors with different codons assembled in one cycle.
Result: ThrA-Gly433Phe mutant best, 48-fold increase.
Seamless assembly crucial for in-frame fusion.
methods: PS-Brick with HindIII (Type IIP) and MlyI (Type IIS).
Codon saturation mutagenesis via primer design.
Shake flask fermentation for threonine measurement.
metrics: Threonine titer: 48-fold increase with Gly433Phe mutant
failures_or_limits: Internal MlyI sites in pACYC184 had to be removed before use.

section_gists.heading: Identification of the metabolic bottleneck for threonine synthesis
section_gists.gist: Second DBTL cycle: identify bottleneck after thrABC overexpression. Five candidate genes (ppc, aspA, aspC, asd, pntAB) assembled into previous vector via PS-Brick. Only asd overexpression increased threonine (56.7%). Others decreased production (e.g., ppc -60.8%). asd identified as bottleneck; negative effects of overexpressing non-rate-limiting steps noted.
process_signals: Hypothesis: unknown bottleneck after thrABC overexpression.
Tested five genes in parallel using PS-Brick.
Result: asd is bottleneck; others detrimental.
Learning: overexpression of non-rate-limiting steps can decrease production due to futile cycles.
Iterative PS-Brick allowed sequential addition of genes.
methods: PS-Brick assembly of ppc, aspA, aspC, asd, pntAB into pACYC184-thrA^433 BC.
Transcriptional analysis by RT-PCR.
Shake flask fermentation.
failures_or_limits: Overexpression of ppc, aspC, aspA, pntAB decreased threonine, indicating they are not bottlenecks and cause futile cycles.

section_gists.heading: Prioritization of isoenzymes for threonine efflux
section_gists.gist: Third DBTL cycle: prioritize threonine exporters. Four exporters (rhtA, rhtB, rhtC, yeaS) assembled with same promoter (P_T) and RBS (BCD1) via two PS-Brick cycles. Seamless assembly enabled precise BCD1-exporter fusion. rhtC overexpression gave highest threonine (3.7 g/L from 0.9 g/L).
process_signals: Goal: identify best exporter for threonine efflux.
Used mid-strength promoter and BCD1 RBS to avoid growth inhibition.
Two PS-Brick cycles: first insert CDS, then add promoter-RBS.
Seamless assembly critical for BCD1 fusion (UAAUG overlap).
Result: rhtC best, 3.7 g/L threonine.
methods: PS-Brick with HindIII/MlyI.
Bicistronic design (BCD1) for translation initiation.
Shake flask fermentation.
metrics: Threonine titer with rhtC: 3.7 g/L (from 0.9 g/L control)

section_gists.heading: Construction of a CRISPR array containing sequence repeats
section_gists.gist: TA clone/BciVI-based PS-Brick used to assemble repetitive sgRNA arrays for CRISPR-Cas9 deletion of tdh and ilvA (threonine catabolism). Double sgRNA array assembled in two cycles. Triple array assembled but triple mutant not obtained. Double mutant strain achieved; fed-batch produced 45.71 g/L threonine.
process_signals: Challenge: assembly of repetitive sequences (sgRNA arrays) fails with homology-based methods.
Solution: PS-Brick with BciVI (TA cloning) and HindIII.
Assembled double sgRNA array for tdh and ilvA deletion.
Triple array assembled but triple mutant not screened.
Double mutant used for fed-batch, achieving 45.71 g/L threonine.
methods: PS-Brick with HindIII/BciVI (TA clone variant).
CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing with pCas/pTarget system.
Fed-batch fermentation.
failures_or_limits: Triple sgRNA array assembled but triple mutant not obtained.

section_gists.heading: Construction of the heterologous 1-propanol pathway
section_gists.gist: Heterologous 1-propanol pathway (kivD and ADH2) assembled into threonine-producing vector via one PS-Brick cycle. Fed-batch produced 1.35 g/L 1-propanol, with accumulation of threonine (21.01 g/L) and 2-ketobutyrate (0.89 g/L), indicating low KivD/ADH2 activity.
process_signals: Extension to 1-propanol production from threonine.
Pathway: kivD (L. lactis) and ADH2 (S. cerevisiae) under P_trc and BCD1.
Assembled in one PS-Brick cycle.
Result: 1.35 g/L 1-propanol, but precursors accumulated, suggesting enzyme limitation.
methods: PS-Brick assembly.
Fed-batch fermentation.
failures_or_limits: Low 1-propanol titer due to insufficient KivD/ADH2 activity; need better enzymes.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: PS-Brick is first method combining Type IIP and IIS REs for iterative, seamless, repetitive assembly. Addresses limitations of BioBrick (scars), Golden Gate (reusability), MoClo/Golden Braid (complexity). Advantages: high efficiency/accuracy, low cost, speed, simplicity. Demonstrated in threonine DBTL cycles and 1-propanol pathway. Threonine titer 45.71 g/L; 1-propanol titer low (1.35 g/L) compared to 10.8 g/L in literature. Future work: biosensors, chromosomal integration, combinatorial strategies.
process_signals: Reflection on method development: PS-Brick fills gap for simple, iterative, seamless, repetitive assembly.
Comparison with other methods: BioBrick (not seamless), Golden Gate (not iterative), MoClo/Golden Braid (complex).
Advantages: robust REs give high efficiency/accuracy; no need for phosphorylated/dephosphorylated DNA; fast (half-day).
Application success: threonine 45.71 g/L with minimal modifications.
Limitation: 1-propanol titer low; need enzyme improvement.
Future: integrate with advanced metabolic engineering tools.
metrics: Threonine: 45.71 g/L (this study) vs. 82.4 g/L (literature max for E. coli)
failures_or_limits: 1-propanol titer low due to enzyme activity.
PS-Brick requires removal of internal RE sites.

section_gists.heading: Conclusions
section_gists.gist: PS-Brick is the first RE-assisted method using both Type IIP and IIS REs, combining iterative and seamless strengths. It is efficient, simple, and addresses three desirable features. Valuable addition to synthetic biology and metabolic engineering toolbox.
process_signals: Summary of achievement: PS-Brick successfully developed and applied.
Key features: iterative, seamless, repetitive assembly.
Positioning: first method to combine both RE types.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The initial problem was the lack of a simple DNA assembly method that is simultaneously iterative, seamless, and capable of handling repetitive sequences, which are all needed for efficient DBTL cycles in metabolic engineering.

The hypothesis was that combining Type IIP REs (for iterative assembly via reusable sites) and Type IIS REs (for seamless assembly via custom overhangs) could achieve all three properties in a single method.

The development followed a design-build-test cycle: design vectors with entrance sites, test cutting/ligation efficiency, optimize to reduce background (uncut plasmid, self-ligation), and validate with multiple use cases.

The application to threonine production was structured as sequential DBTL cycles, each addressing a specific metabolic engineering target (feedback resistance, bottleneck, export, catabolism), demonstrating the iterative power of PS-Brick.
The method was extended to challenging scenarios: repetitive CRISPR arrays and heterologous pathway assembly, showing versatility.
Limitations encountered (internal RE sites, self-ligation, low 1-propanol titer) were acknowledged and partially addressed, with suggestions for future improvements.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Whether the triple CRISPR mutant could be obtained with further optimization.
The exact efficiency improvement after dephosphorylation (not quantified in text).
Whether PS-Brick can be automated or scaled for high-throughput assembly.
The performance of PS-Brick with very large constructs or multiple fragments.
The generalizability to other organisms beyond E. coli.

events.summary: Current DNA assembly methods lack a simple technique that is simultaneously iterative, seamless, and capable of handling repetitive sequences, which are needed for efficient DBTL cycles in metabolic engineering.
Combining Type IIP and IIS restriction endonucleases in a single assembly method can achieve iterative, seamless, and repetitive sequence assembly.
PS-Brick design uses Type IIS REs BmrI (1 nt overhang) and MlyI (blunt end) combined with Type IIP RE SphI to create reusable entrance sites for iterative assembly.
PS-Brick was applied to threonine production through iterative DBTL cycles, achieving 45.71 g/L threonine in fed-batch fermentation.
Seamless cloning with PS-Brick enabled precise in-frame fusion for codon saturation mutagenesis and bicistronic design, and repetitive sequence cloning enabled tandem CRISPR sgRNA arrays.
Heterologous 1-propanol pathway was assembled in one PS-Brick cycle, yielding 1.35 g/L 1-propanol in fed-batch fermentation.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk covers the abstract and background, introducing the problem, hypothesis, design, and initial validation of PS-Brick. Events focus on the method's development and application highlights.

events.summary: Design of PS-Brick using Taq polymerase to add dA overhangs for TA cloning with BmrI-digested vector.
Design of PS-Brick using high-fidelity polymerase for blunt-end ligation with MlyI-digested vector.
Donor PCR fragments in PS-Brick are single-end flanked by adjacent Type IIP and Type IIS restriction sites, unlike BioBrick.
Optimization of PS-Brick: testing cutting efficiency of BmrI and MlyI over time.
Investigation of SphI digestion time effect on assembly efficiency and accuracy.
numbers_or_metrics: SphI digestion from 15 min to 180 min

events.summary: Comparison of PS-Brick efficiency and accuracy with traditional Type IIP RE-based cloning using XbaI and SphI.
numbers_or_metrics: Efficiency: ~70,000 CFU/μg for XbaI, ~65,000 for BmrI, ~68,000 for MlyI; Accuracy: 85% XbaI, 90% BmrI, 88% MlyI

events.summary: Testing effect of 5'-phosphorylation of non-cut PCR end on PS-Brick efficiency and accuracy.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains design details, optimization experiments, and comparison data for PS-Brick.

events.summary: PS-Brick design uses both Type IIP and IIS REs, unlike previous methods that use only one type.
PS-Brick can use any Type IIP RE generating sticky ends, providing broad options.
PS-Brick uses Type IIS REs generating single base overhangs or blunt ends to match PCR products.
Donor PCR fragments in PS-Brick are flanked on only one end by restriction sites, unlike BioBrick and Golden Gate.
Seamless character of PS-Brick achieved via blunt end cloning or tailorable TA cloning combining PCR and Type IIS REs.
Cutting efficiencies of BmrI and MlyI tested by gel electrophoresis and transformation test; 30 min digestion chosen.
SphI digestion time (15-180 min) did not affect assembly efficiency (~4-8×10^4 CFU/μg DNA) or accuracy (88.2-95.1%).

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains detailed design principles and optimization experiments for PS-Brick, directly relevant to the paper's core method development.

events.summary: 20 ThrA saturation mutants generated by one PS-Brick cycle; Gly433Phe mutant gave 48-fold higher threonine.
Five candidate genes tested for bottleneck after thrABC overexpression; only asd increased threonine (56.7%), others decreased production.
asd identified as metabolic bottleneck after thrABC overexpression; negative effects of overexpressing non-rate-limiting steps noted.
Four threonine exporters prioritized; rhtC overexpression gave highest threonine (3.7 g/L from 0.9 g/L).
Double sgRNA array for tdh and ilvA deletion assembled via TA clone/BciVI PS-Brick; double mutant strain obtained.
Fed-batch fermentation of double mutant strain produced 45.71 g/L threonine, 28.7% higher than control.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers key experimental results: saturation mutagenesis, bottleneck identification, exporter prioritization, CRISPR array assembly, fed-batch threonine production, and 1-propanol pathway assembly. All events are directly supported by quotes.

events.summary: Heterologous 1-propanol pathway assembled in one PS-Brick cycle and tested in fed-batch fermentation, yielding 1.35 g/L 1-propanol but with precursor accumulation indicating low enzyme activity.
Low 1-propanol titer attributed to insufficient activity of heterologous KivD and ADH2 enzymes.
Future work should focus on obtaining more efficient 2-keto-acid decarboxylase and alcohol dehydrogenase.
PS-Brick is the first restriction-ligation assembly method using both Type IIP and IIS REs, combining iterative and seamless strengths.
PS-Brick addresses three key requirements: iterative assembly, seamless assembly, and repetitive sequence assembly.
PS-Brick demonstrated for iterative DBTL cycles in threonine overproduction, achieving 45.71 g/L threonine by deleting two genes and overexpressing two genes and one operon.
PS-Brick is unfit for combinatorial pathway engineering due to limitations in multi-part assembly.
The need to eliminate internal restriction sites remains a general limitation for RE-based strategies, including PS-Brick.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers heterologous 1-propanol pathway assembly and results, discussion of PS-Brick features, advantages, limitations, and conclusions. Multiple process events extracted.

events.summary: Construction of original PS-Brick vectors pOB and pOM from pUC19 by removing internal BmrI and MlyI sites and inserting truncated mCherry fragments with entrance sites.
Construction of pOthr vector for metabolic engineering by removing MlyI sites from pACYC184 and inserting truncated thrABC operon with HindIII/MlyI entrance sites.
Workflow of PS-Brick assembly using SphI/MlyI entrance site: PCR amplification, SphI digestion of donor, double digestion of vector, ligation, and transformation.
numbers_or_metrics: fivefold molar excess of insert DNA

events.summary: PS-Brick assembly with HindIII/MlyI entrance site using vector pOthr, same conditions as SphI/MlyI workflow.
PS-Brick assembly with SphI/BmrI and HindIII/BciVI entrance sites using Ex-Taq polymerase for donor amplification.
CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing procedure for deleting tdh and ilvA in MG1655 using pCas9 and ptargetET-tdh-ilvA.
Fed-batch fermentation protocol for threonine and 1-propanol production in a bioreactor with controlled pH, DO, and glucose feeding.
Analytical methods for quantifying biomass, gene expression, glucose, amino acids, 2-ketobutyrate, and 1-propanol.

evidence_bank.quote: DNA assembly scenarios encountered in metabolic engineering for the construction of amino acid and related compound producers, such as design-build-test-learn cycles, construction of precise genetic circuits and repetitive DNA molecules, usually require for iterative, scarless and repetitive sequence assembly methods, respectively.

Here, we developed a Type IIP and IIS RE-assisted method named PS-Brick that comprehensively takes advantage of the properties of PCR fragments and REs for iterative, seamless and repetitive sequence assembly.
BmrI (generating 1 nt cohesive end) and MlyI (generating blunt end) are used as Type IIS REs for the proof-of-concept of the PS-Brick assembly strategy (Fig. 1bc).

An application of metabolic engineering to threonine production, including the release of feedback regulation, elimination of metabolic bottlenecks, intensification of threonine export and inactivation of threonine catabolism, was stepwise resolved in E. coli by rounds of “design-build-test-learn” cycles through the iterative PS-Brick paradigm, and 45.71 g/L threonine was obtained through fed-batch fermentation.

seamless cloning enabled precise in-frame fusion for codon saturation mutagenesis and bicistronic design, and the repetitive sequence cloning ability of PS-Brick enabled construction of tandem CRISPR sgRNA arrays for genome editing.

the heterologous pathway deriving 1-propanol pathway from threonine, composed of Lactococcus lactis kivD and Saccharomyces cerevisiae ADH2, was assembled by one cycle of PS-Brick, resulting in 1.35 g/L 1-propanol in fed-batch fermentation.
Taq polymerase can add a single non template-directed deoxyadenosine (dA) residue to the 3' end of duplex PCR products
The blunt end of donor PCR fragments amplified by high-fidelity polymerase can be ligated to the blunt end of MlyI digested vector pOM
donor PCR fragments in PS-Brick are only single-end flanked by adjacent Type IIP and Type IIS restriction
The transform efficiency of Bmrl digested pOB vector and Mlyl digested pOM vector
The second cutting efficiency of Sphl was investigated through the assembly efficiency and correct ligation rate
Comparison of the efficiency and accuracy between PS-Brick and the traditional Type IIP RE-based clone method with XbaI and Sphl
The effect 5'-phosphorylation of the non-cut PCR end on the efficiency and accuracy of PS-Brick
Two types of REs (Type IIP and IIS) are used in PS-Brick, whereas only one Type of REs is applied in BioBrick-like standards or Golden Gate-like assembly, respectively.
PS-Brick can use all the Type IIP REs cleaving to sticky end, which provides broad options.
The PS-Brick standard adopts Type IIS endonucleases generating single base overhangs or blunt ends to match the PCR products with an unpaired dA extension or a blunt end, respectively
The donor PCR fragments in PS-Brick are only flanked on one end by restriction sites, allowing for simultaneous utilization of the properties of PCR and REs
The seamless character of PS-Brick is achieved via blunt end cloning or tailorable TA cloning combining the properties of PCR and Type IIS REs.
The cutting efficiencies of the Type IIS REs BmrI and MlyI presented in vectors pOB and pOM were tested through gel electrophoresis with a cutting time ranging from 15 to 180 min.
The accuracy of PS-Brick assembly was measured by DNA sequencing of 20 clones and ranged from 88.2 to 95.1%
the thrA-Gly433Phe mutant resulted in the highest concentration of threonine, 48-fold higher than that of the wild-type control
only overexpression of the asd gene led to a 56.7% increase in threonine production
The asd gene encoding aspartyl semialdehyde dehydrogenase was first demonstrated as the metabolic bottleneck after thrABC overexpression in this study
upregulation of the non-rate-limiting reaction step of pathway inversely decreases threonine accumulation
the overexpression of the rhtC gene increased threonine production from 0.9 g/L to 3.7 g/L
the double mutation strain E. coli MG1655△tdh△ilvA was obtained

Batch culture of this engineered strain in a 7.5-L fermenter allowed production of 45.71 g/L threonine, 28.7% higher than the production of control strains retaining the tdh and ilvA genes (35.52 g/L)
the precursor threonine and 2-keto-butyrate accumulated at relatively high concentrations of 21.01 g/L and 0.89 g/L, respectively
which suggests that the activity of heterologous KivD and ADH2 was not high enough.
In future work, further effort should be made to obtain 2-keto-acid decarboxylase and alcohol dehydrogenase with high efficiency.
the PS-Brick framework is the first restriction-ligation assembly method using both Type IIP and IIS REs
we have developed a novel PS-Brick framework that enables iterative, seamless and sequence-repeat DNA assembly.
45.71 g/L threonine was obtained by deleting two genes and overexpressing two genes and one operon.
PS-Brick is unfit for combinatorial pathway engineering because of its limitations in multi-part assembly.
The need for elimination of internal restriction sites is still a general limitation for the RE-based strategies of DNA assembly.
One BmrI site and three MlyI sites located in the pUC19 vector backbones are removed through overlap extension PCR
the newly obtained vectors pOB and pOM from pUC19, containing the entrance sites SphI/MlyI and SphI/BmrI, respectively, were taken as the original PS-Brick vectors
Three MlyI sites located in the pACYC184 vector backbones were mutated through overlap extension PCR
Another MlyI site located in the MCS sequence of the pACYC184 vector was removed by HindIII and NruI digestion.
the newly obtained vector pOthr containing adjacent HindIII/MlyI entrance sites was used for subsequent metabolic pathway assembly.
The purified PCR products were digested with SphI only for 30 min, heat-inactivated at 60 °C for 20 min and then column-purified for ligation.
The original PS-Brick vector pOM containing the SphI/MlyI entrance site was cleaved by SphI and MlyI for 30 min in the same buffer.
Except for the use of the Type IIP RE HindIII and the original vector pOthr, all the conditions were same as above.
The primers flanked with SphI/BmrI or HindIII/BciVI sites were used to amplify the donor fragment by Ex-Taq DNA Polymerase.
All the other conditions were the same as above except for the REs and the original vectors.
Transformants were identified by colony PCR and DNA sequencing with primer pairs ilvA-I-F/R and tdh-I-F/R for ilvA and tdh, respectively.
Dissolved oxygen tension was maintained at 50% of air saturation by automatically cascading with stirrer speeds ranging from 200 to 1000 rpm.
The concentration of glucose over all of the fed-batch cultures was maintained within the range of 10±5 g/L by supplying 700 g/L of glucose reservoir.
The concentration of glucose was assayed with an enzyme electrode analyser (SBA-40D; Institute of Biology, Shandong, China) containing glucose oxidase.
A feedback-resistant ThrA variant (Gly433Arg) derived from the threonine hyper-producer ATCC 21277 [50] has been widely used for the production of threonine and its derivatives [51–53].
Remarkably, the thrA-Gly433Phe mutant resulted in the highest concentration of threonine, 48-fold higher than that of the wild-type control.

Second, P $_{T}$ and BCD1 [65, 66] were amplified as donor PCR fragments, and inserted in front of the ATG start codon of the four exporter genes in the last round assembled vectors, generating the corresponding vectors pACYC184-thrA $^{433}$ BC-asd-P $_{T}$ BCD1-rhtA/rhtB/rhtC/yeaS (Fig.

Four threonine exporters under the same standard initiation element (a mid-strength promoter (P $_{T}$ ) from a constitutive bacteriophage P $_{L}$ promoter library [65] along with a mid-strength bicistronic design (BCD1) RBS [66]) were assembled into the last round PS-Brick plasmids pACYC184-thrA $^{433}$ BC-asd through two cycles of PS-Brick reaction, respectively.

Although MoClo and Golden Braid variants brought breakthroughs into the Golden Gate assembly that enabled iterative workflows, they require substantial efforts to prepare elaborate assembly vectors and complex protocols $[12, 32]$ , which poses a barrier to their widespread adoption $[42]$ .
MoClo and Golden Braid variants brought breakthroughs to Golden Gate assembly that enable full reusability of composite parts $[12, 31, 32]$ .

A third distinctive characteristic of the PS-Brick scheme is its simplicity and convenience: sophisticated Golden Braid and MoClo frameworks required elaborate plasmid libraries to prepare donor DNA in the defined format [12, 32], which poses a barrier to their straightforward application and widespread adoption.

The increased activity of anaplerotic phosphoenolpyruvate carboxylase coupled with the simultaneous activity of gluconeogenic reactions will make up ATP-dissipating futile cycles [59], which was assumed to deteriorate threonine synthesis with its high energetic costs [60].

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Lack of a simple DNA assembly method that is simultaneously iterative, seamless, and capable of handling repetitive sequences for efficient DBTL cycles in metabolic engineering.

canonical_events.summary: Current DNA assembly methods (BioBrick, Golden Gate, MoClo/Golden Braid) lack a simple technique that is simultaneously iterative, seamless, and capable of handling repetitive sequences, needed for efficient DBTL cycles in metabolic engineering.
Combining Type IIP and IIS restriction endonucleases in a single assembly method (PS-Brick) can achieve iterative, seamless, and repetitive sequence assembly.

PS-Brick design uses Type IIS REs BmrI (1 nt overhang) and MlyI (blunt end) combined with Type IIP RE SphI to create reusable entrance sites for iterative assembly. Vectors pOB and pOM constructed from pUC19.

Optimization of PS-Brick: cutting efficiency of BmrI and MlyI tested; 30 min digestion chosen to reduce uncut plasmid background. SphI digestion time (15-180 min) did not affect assembly efficiency (~4-8×10^4 CFU/μg DNA) or accuracy (88.2-95.1%).
Self-ligation of vector due to incomplete SphI digestion identified as cause of incorrect clones; dephosphorylation suggested to prevent self-ligation.

Comparison of PS-Brick with traditional Type IIP RE-based cloning (XbaI/SphI) showed similar efficiency (~65,000-70,000 CFU/μg) and accuracy (85-90%). Blunt end/MlyI-based PS-Brick is completely seamless and sequence-independent; TA clone/BmrI-based PS-Brick offers more RE options.

First DBTL cycle: Seamless PS-Brick used for codon saturation mutagenesis of ThrA residue 433 to relieve feedback inhibition. 20 mutants generated in one cycle; ThrA-Gly433Phe gave 48-fold higher threonine than wild-type.

Second DBTL cycle: Five candidate genes (ppc, aspA, aspC, asd, pntAB) tested for bottleneck after thrABC overexpression. Only asd overexpression increased threonine (56.7%); others decreased production (e.g., ppc -60.8%). asd identified as bottleneck.

Third DBTL cycle: Four threonine exporters (rhtA, rhtB, rhtC, yeaS) prioritized using bicistronic design (BCD1) and mid-strength promoter. rhtC overexpression gave highest threonine (3.7 g/L from 0.9 g/L).

Fourth DBTL cycle: Repetitive sgRNA arrays for tdh and ilvA deletion assembled via TA clone/BciVI PS-Brick. Double mutant strain obtained; fed-batch produced 45.71 g/L threonine, 28.7% higher than control.

Heterologous 1-propanol pathway (kivD and ADH2) assembled in one PS-Brick cycle. Fed-batch produced 1.35 g/L 1-propanol, but precursors accumulated (threonine 21.01 g/L, 2-ketobutyrate 0.89 g/L), indicating low enzyme activity.
Low 1-propanol titer attributed to insufficient activity of heterologous KivD and ADH2 enzymes; future work should focus on obtaining more efficient enzymes.

PS-Brick is unfit for combinatorial pathway engineering due to limitations in multi-part assembly, and the need to eliminate internal restriction sites remains a general limitation for RE-based strategies.

PS-Brick successfully demonstrated for iterative DBTL cycles in threonine overproduction, achieving 45.71 g/L threonine by deleting two genes and overexpressing two genes and one operon. PS-Brick is the first restriction-ligation assembly method using both Type IIP and IIS REs, combining iterative and seamless strengths.

edges.reason: The problem motivates the hypothesis for a new method.
The hypothesis leads to the design of PS-Brick.
The design is tested and optimized experimentally.
Optimization data allows comparison with traditional cloning.
The designed method is applied to the first DBTL cycle.
Sequential DBTL cycle: after feedback resistance, identify bottleneck.
Sequential DBTL cycle: after bottleneck, intensify export.
Sequential DBTL cycle: after export, inactivate catabolism.
Extension to 1-propanol production from threonine platform.
Low titer reveals limitation of enzyme activity.
High threonine titer validates the method's effectiveness.
1-propanol assembly demonstrates versatility, though with limitations.
Self-ligation issue contributes to general limitation of RE-based methods.
Comparison shows PS-Brick performs similarly to traditional methods while offering additional benefits.

must_cover_points: Problem: lack of simple iterative, seamless, repetitive assembly method.
Hypothesis: combining Type IIP and IIS REs.
Design of PS-Brick vectors with entrance sites.
Optimization of digestion conditions and efficiency/accuracy metrics.
Application to threonine DBTL cycles: feedback resistance, bottleneck identification, export prioritization, catabolism inactivation.
Key quantitative results: 48-fold increase with ThrA mutant, 56.7% increase with asd, 3.7 g/L with rhtC, 45.71 g/L final threonine titer.
Repetitive sequence assembly for CRISPR arrays.
1-propanol pathway assembly and low titer limitation.
Validation of PS-Brick as first method combining both RE types for iterative seamless assembly.
